titleStmt.title: O. Leid. 681: Α letter attesting the Latin loanword expungere

encodingDesc.ab: Converted from a Word document for Pylon.

docTitle.titlePart: O. Leid. 681: Α letter attesting the Latin loanword expungere

div.p:
This ostracon, which is partly transcribed as a descriptum in O.Leid., contains a letter fragment.
See Bagnall, Sijpesteijn and Worp 1980: 184. The edition of the ostracon is based on the digital photo, which is available at https://papyri.info/ddbdp/o.leid;;681/images. The sherd appears to be broken on all sides. The convex side has a reddish-brown color and preserves six incomplete lines in a fairly legible script, with only a few letters being slightly faded, while the concave is blank. The script points to a date between the third and fourth century.

On the basis of the preserved text, we can only make conjectures about the content of the ostracon, knowing only that the sender dispatched some items to the addressee (ll. 5–6). We may assume that there is also content of a military nature owing to the rare word ἐξπουνγεύεσθαι, which is the loanword used in military contexts for Latin expungere (“to strike off” the name of a soldier from the list of those on active duty, that is, “to discharge” a soldier).
On Latin loanwords in Greek see Dickey 2023; for an overview of the loanwords used for expungo and its derivatives see ibid. s.v. ἐξπούγγευσις, ἐξπουγγεύω, ἐξπούγκερος, ἐξπούγκτωρ. This task was the responsibility of the expunctor, an official of the Roman army charged with the discharge of soldiers. It can also carry the general meaning “to check off” or “to strike off,” i.e. items on a list.
See L&S s.v. expungo. See also the notes to O.Claud. 2 258.4–5 (A.D. half II), P.Oxy. 9 1204.16 (A.D. 299), P.Kell. 1 21.9 (A.D. 321), and Rom.Mil.Rec. 47 col. 1, 16 (A.D. 223–235). Expungo in the sense of “to check off” can likewise be associated with the practice of checking off soldiers who were destined to receive an award.
Cf. L&S s.v. expungo B and the cited example from Tertullian (Tert. Cor. Mil. 1 expungebantur milites laureati).

In our text, it is unclear whether the infinitive carries the specific sense of discharging a soldier or the more general meaning of “checking off” or “striking off” items on a list. In papyri and ostraca, however, the verb and its derivatives are attested in military contexts: O.Claud. 2 258.4–5 (A.D. half II): ἐξ|πουγεύσαμεν των (l. τὸ) δονάτιβον); P.Oxy. 9 1204.6–7 (A.D. 299): πρὸς ἐκσφούνγευσιν τῶν ἐκεῖσε διακειμένων στρατιωτῶν, l. 19: ἐκσφουνγεύειν τοὺς στρατιώτας; BGU 2 435.14 (A.D. ΙΙ–ΙΙΙ): ἐξπούγκερος; P.Kell. 1 21.8–9 (A.D. 321): τοὺς π̣αρεπιδημοῦντας στρατιώ|[τας καὶ ὀφ]φικιαλίους ἐκσπούν̣κτορας (l. ἐξπούγκτορας); P.Kell. 1 77.13 (A.D. IV): ἐκσφουγ̣[κτορ-.
Cf. also the Latin papyri CLTP 3 222.1-4 (A.D. III): Summa Orontrasium LXX̣ II | absen[tes] VIII | summa aegrorum I[I] | reliqui expu[nge]n[t]ur `LXV[I]´ LX[I]I ; Rom.Mil.Rec. 47 col. 1, 16 (A.D. 223–235): [ - ca. 8 -] Ṭ[iminiu]ṣ P̣ạ[u]liṇus dec(urio) adṃissa proṇ[u]ṇṭ[iavit - ca.23 -] iiii Kal(endas) April(es) expungẹnṭur. Accordingly, we may reasonably assume that either the recipient, the sender, or someone known to them — perhaps the person referred to by (οὐ) κομψῶς ἔχει in l. 4 — was in military service.

note.p: ] παρὰ Σ̣α̣ρ̣ [ ̣ ̣] ̣[: The word following παρά is most likely a personal name in the genitive. Since the script shows an upward inclination, the visible trace at the end of the first line must be the descender of χ, ρ, φ, or ι. Two or three letters are missing in the lacuna. Σ̣α̣ρ̣[απ]ί̣[ωνος (TM Nam 5663) fits well, but there are other possibilities.

]ου κομψῶς ἔχει: Various proposals may be considered in attempting to restore the beginning of l. 4. There appears to be a trace of the upper end of a σ joined to an ο, perhaps the pronoun σου (i.e. σου κομψῶς ἔχει). The sender could thus be informing the recipient that one of his relatives is well, or unwell if the negative οὐ is to be read (οὐ κομψῶς ἔχει).

] ̣ι μου ὅτι ἔπεμψά σοι [: In all likelihood, the letter preceding ι is either κ or α. Although the ending -αι (for the vocatives κύριαί μου or ἀδελφαί μου)
For the interchange of ε and αι see Gignac 1976: 193. may at first sight appear tempting, the combination κι is the stronger candidate. From a paleographical point of view, the ligature between the two letters does not appear straight, unlike other attestations of αι written by the hand of the scribe (cf. the αι of γυναικί, ἐξπουνγεύεσθαι and κ]αί). If -κι is correct, then a possible restoration for the beginning of l. 5 would be τῇ γυναι]κ̣ί μου, most likely in the sense of "tell my wife that I sent to you."

ὅτι ἔπεμψά σοι | [ ca. ? κ]αὶ βησὶν φύνικος̣ φ̣ ̣[: Since the sense of the subordinate clause ὅτι ἔπεμψά σοι continues to the next line we can assume that probably one to two items are lost in the missing text between ll. 5 and 6. However, the sender might have also mentioned the name of the person carrying the items (e.g. with a διά/μετά + name prepositional adverbial phrase). βησσίον “cup” is rare in the papyri and is found again as βησίν in P.Ross. Georg. 2 41.42 (A.D. end II–III, βησίν [l. βησσίον] ἱάλου [l. ὑάλου]), as βησίον in SPP 20 67 V col.1, 4 (A.D. 199, βησίον κασσιτερ(ινὸν) α), and as βισίν in SB 26 16831.9 (A.D. IV, βισίν [l. βισίον] μέλιτος) (cf. also ἁλατοβήσσιον in Coptic [Sahidic] CPR 12 14.4 [A.D. VII–VIII]).

For the interchange of υ and οι in φύνικος (l. φοίνικος) see Gignac 1967: 197. After the letter φ at the end of the line, a trace of a downward straight stroke is visible, which may be the lower part of an ι. Usually, in the papyri, dates are accompanied by an adjective indicating whether they are dried (ξηρός), green (χλωροί), juicy (πατητοί) or nut-like (καρυωτοί) (cf. Clarysse 2018: 224). However, in attempting to restore the end of the line with any of these adjectives, the visible letter following φύνικος is somewhat smudged and appears to resemble a φ. One might try to read ξ for ξηρός — since π, χ, and κ do not match the letter’s shape at all — on the basis of comparison with the less clearly written ξ of ἐξπουνγεύεσθαι; but the smudged letter’s downstroke leans leftwards rather than rightwards, as in the φ of φύνικος.

listBibl.bibl: Bagnall, R.S., Sijpesteijn, P.J., and Worp, K.A. (1980) Greek Ostraka. a Catalogue of the Greek Ostraka in the National Museum of Antiquities at Leiden, with a Chapter on the Greek Ostraka in the Papyrological Institute of the University of Leiden. Zutphen.
Bingen, J. et. alii (1997) Mons Claudianus. Ostraca graeca et latina II (O. Claud. 191 à 416). Cairo.
Clarysse, W. (2018) "Egypt," in P. Erdkamp and C. Holleran (eds.), The Routledge Handbook of Diet and Nutrition in the Roman World, London – New York.
Dickey, E. (2023) Latin Loanwords in Ancient Greek. A Lexicon and Analysis. Cambridge.
Fink, R.O. (1971) Roman Military Records on Papyrus. Cleveland.
Gignac, F.T. (1976) A Grammar of the Greek Papyri of the Roman and Byzantine Periods. I. Phonology. Milano.
Hunt, A.S. (1912) The Oxyrhynchus papyri. IX (Nos. 1166–1223), edited with translation and notes. London.
Worp, K.A. (1995) Greek Papyri from Kellis I: (P. Kell. G.) Nos. 1–90. Exeter.

div.ab:
παρὰ Σαρ
τῇ γυναικί μου ε
ἐξπουνγεύεσθαι πε
ου κομψῶς ἔχει κε
ι μου ὅτι ἔπεμψά σοι
καὶ βησίονβησὶν φοίνικοςφύνικος φ

availability.p: © Heidelberger Gesamtverzeichnis der griechischen Papyrusurkunden Ägyptens. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 License.

sourceDesc.p: The contents of this document are generated from SOSOL.

div.p: […] to be discharged (?) […] he (or she) is (un)well. […] that I sent to you […] and a cup of dates […]
